# 6.1 Why Visualize Data? {.unnumbered}

“A picture is worth a thousand words,” and in research, figures and graphs can be incredibly useful. They help us see patterns that might be difficult to notice in a table.

Visualizations can help you answer questions like:

- What does the distribution look like?
- Are values clustered or spread out?
- Are there outliers?
- Are there unexpected categories?
- Do groups look similar or different?
- Does something look wrong with the data?

Remember: it is incredibly important to visualize your data. You never know what descriptive statistics may be hiding.

## Graphs Can Reveal Problems

Imagine two variables have the same mean and standard deviation. You might assume they look similar. But one could be normally distributed, another could be skewed, and another could have a few extreme outliers.

The numbers are important, but they are not enough by themselves.

## Graphs Support Interpretation

Visualizations are also useful for communication. A well-chosen graph can help readers understand your data quickly. A poorly chosen graph can confuse readers or imply something the data do not support.

The goal is not to make a graph because a graph is required. The goal is to choose a graph that helps you understand or communicate something meaningful about the data.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common Mistake"}
Do not choose a graph only because it looks nice. Choose a graph because it matches the variable type and helps answer the question you are asking.
:::
